title: 第 33 節
en: Sanskrit commentaries. If we take for instance the story of Janaka, who promised a large prize to the wisest Brahman at his sacrifice, and compare this story, as it is given in the Satapatha-brahmana (xi. 4. 6.) with the third Adhyaya of the Brihadaraiiyaka where the same subject occurs, we find in the Aranyaka all the details given almost in the same words as in the Brahmana, but enlarged with so many additions, particularly with respect to the philosophical disputations which take place between Yajnavalkya and the other Brahmans, that we cannot hesitate for a moment to consider the Aranyaka as an enlargement upon the Brahmana.

The chief interest which the Aranyakas possess at the present moment consists in their philosophy. The philosophical chapters well known under the name of Upanishads are almost the only portion of Vedic literature which is extensively read to this day. They contain, or are supposed to contain, the highest authority on which the various systems of philosophy in India rest. Not only the Vedanta philosopher, who, by his very name, professes his faith in the ends and objects of the Veda,1 but the Sankhya, the Vaiseshika, the Nyaya, and Yoga philosophers, all pretend to find in the Upanishads some warranty for their tenets, however antagonistic in their bearing. The same applies to the numerous sects that have existed and still exist in India. Their founders, if they have 1 Vedanta is used, but not yet in its technical sense, Taittiriyaaranyaka, x. 12. ; a verse frequently repeated elsewhere.

ft ^u^fg ircm^T^ M<njdi: irf^p^r *"in UPANI SHADS. 317 any pretensions to orthodoxy, invariably appeal to some passage in the Upanishads in order to substantiate their own reasonings. Now it is true that in the Upanishads themselves there is so much freedom and breadth of thought that it is not difficult to find in them some authority for almost any shade of philosophical opinion. The old Upanishads did not pretend to give more than u guesses at truth," and when, in course of time, they became invested with an inspired character, they allowed great latitude to those who professed to believe in them as revelation. Yet this was not sufficient for the rank growth of philosophical doctrines during the latter ages of Indian history ; and when none of the ancient Upanishads could be found to suit the purpose, the founders of new sects had no scruple and no difficulty in composing new Upanishads of their own. This accounts for the large and ever growing number of these treatises. Every new collection of MSS., every new list of Upanishads given by native writers, adds to the number of those which were known before; and the most modern compilations seem now to enjoy the same authority as the really genuine treatises.

The original Upanishads had their place in the Aranyakas and Brahmanas. There is only one instance of a Sanhita containing Upanishads — the Vajasaneyi-sanhita, which comprises the Isa-upanishad, forming the 40th book, and the Sivasankalpa, forming part of the 34th book. This, however, so far from proving the greater antiquity of that Upanishad, only serves to confirm the modern date of the whole collection known under the name of Vajasa- 318 UPANISHADS.

neyi-sanhita.1 But though the proper place of the genuine Upanishads was in the Brahmanas, and here chiefly in those secondary portions commonly called Aranyakas, yet in later times, the Upanishads obtained a more independent position, and though they still professed to belong more particularly to one or the other of the four Yedas, that relationship became very lax and changeable.

The true etymological meaning of the word Upanishad had been forgotten in India. It is generally explained by rahasya, or guhyd ddesdh, mystery ; and an artificial etymology is given, according to which Upanishad would mean u destruction of passion or ignorance, by means of divine revelation."2 The original signification of the word, however, must have been that of sitting down near somebody in order to listen, or in order to meditate and worship. Thus we find up + sad used in the sense of sitting and worshipping :
Kv. ix. 11. 6. — Namasa it lipa sidata, " Approach him with praise."
Ev. x. 73. 11. — Vayali suparna/h lipa sedur Fndram priyamedhah rishayah na/dhamanah, " The poets with good thoughts have approached Indra begging, like birds with beautiful wings."

The root ds, which has the same meaning as sad, to sit, if joined with the preposition upa, expresses the same idea as upa sad, i. e. to approach respectfully, to worship (Rv. x. 153. 1). It is frequently used to express the position which the pupil occupies 1 Mahidhara maintains that some parts of the Upanishad were aimed at the Buddhists, who denied the existence of an intelligent Self, called life a water bubble, and knowledge intoxication.
2 Colebrooke, Essays, i. 92.

UPANISIIADS. 319 when listening to his teacher,1 and it clearly expresses a position of inferiority in such passages as, Sat.br&hmana, i. 3. 4. 15 : "tasmad upary&sinam kshatriyam adhastad imah praja upasate," " therefore those people below (the Vis or Vaisyas) sit under, or pay respect to the Kshatriya who sits above.,, Still more decisive is another passage in the same work (ix. 4. 3. 3), where upanishddin is used in the sense of subject : " kshatraya tad visam adhastad upanishadinim karoti," " he thus makes the Vis below subject to the Kshatriya." There can be little doubt therefore that Upanishad meant originally the act of sitting down near a teacher,2 of submissively listening to him ; and it is easy to trace the steps by which it came to mean implicit faith,3 and, at last, truth or divine revelation.

The songs of the Veda contained but little of philosophy or theosophy, and what the Brahmans call the higher knowledge is not to be sought for in the hymns of the Rishis. "What,"4 says the author of the Svetasvatara-upanishad, " what shall a man do with the hymns, who does not know that eternal word of the hymns in the highest heaven, that in which all the gods are absorbed ? Those who know it, they are blessed." The same sentiment is fre- 1 Pan. iii. 4. 72. comment. : Upasito gurum bhavan ; and upasito gurur bhavata.
2 In this sense Upanishad is frequently used in the plural, and signifies sessions.
3 Chhandogya-upanishad, i. 1. 9. '4J^'c| fifpfl «RT^"fW ^nnJfafa^l <T^ efH«lTK I " What a man performs with knowledge, trust, and faith, that is effectual."
4 Svetasvatara-upanishad, ed. Roer, Bibliotheca Indica, vii.

quently expressed, but nowhere with greater force than in a passage of the Katha-upanishad1, a passage most remarkable in many respects. " That divine Self," the poet says, " is not to be grasped by tradition2, nor by understanding, nor by all revelation ; by him whom He himself chooses, by him alone is He to be grasped ; that Self chooses his body as his own," Kammohun Roy when he visited the British Museum and found the late Dr. Rosen engaged in preparing an edition of the hymns of the Yeda, expressed his surprise at so useless an undertaking. But the same philosopher looked upon the Upanishads as worthy to become the foundation of a new religion, and he published several of them himself with notes and translations. " The adoration of the invisible Supreme Being," he writes, " is exclusively prescribed by the Upanishads or the principal parts of the Veda, and also by the Vedant," and if other portions of the Veda seem to be in contradiction with the pure doctrine of the Upanishads, he hints that the whole work must not only be stripped of its authority, but looked upon as altogether unintelligible.3 The early Hindus did not find any difficulty in reconciling the most different and sometimes contradictory opinions in their search after truth ; and a most extraordinary medley of oracular sayings might .be collected from the Upanishads, even from those which are genuine and comparatively ancient, all tending to elucidate the darkest points of philosophy and religion, the creation of the world, the nature of 1 II. 23. It is also found in the Mundaka.
tary : " ekavedasvikaranena," " by learning one Veda."
3 Translation of the Kena-upanishad by Rammohun Roy, Calcutta, 1816, p. 6.

UPANISHADS. 321 God, the relation of man to God, and similar subjects. That one statement should be contradicted by another seems never to have been felt as any serious difficulty. Thus we read in the first verse of the Svetasvataraupanishad : " Is Brahman the cause ? Whence are we born ? By what do we live ? Where do we go ? At whose command do we walk after the Law, in happiness and misery ? Is Time the cause, or Nature, or Law, or Chance, or the Elements ? Is Man to be taken as the source of all ? Nor is it their union, because there must be an independent Self, and even that independent Self has no power over that which causes happiness and pain." ! The answers returned to such questions are naturally vague and various. Thus Madhava in his Commentary on Parasara, quotes first from the Bahvricha-upanishad. " In the beginning this (world) was Self alone, there was nothing else winking. He thought, Let me create the worlds, Bnd he created these worlds." From this it would follow that the absolute Self was supposed to have created everything out of nothing. But immediately afterwards Madhava quotes from another Upanishad, the iSvetasvatara (IV. 10.), where Maya or delusion is called the principle, and the Great Lord himself, the deluded.2 This is evidently an 'srfvfefTT: 5N ^%n^5 snfro% "sTuf^fr ^nsrfli Y 322 UPANISHADS.

allusion to Sankhya doctrines, but Madhava explains it in a different sense. He maintains that here also the Divine Self is meant by the Great Lord, and that Delusion is only one of his powers, as heat is a power of fire.1 And he appeals to another passage in the same Upanishad (I. 3.), where it is said " that sages endowed with meditation and intuition, saw the power of the Divine Self, concealed by his own qualities." This same interpretation is adopted in the Sutras of the Vedantaphilosophy, but it by no means follows that therefore it is the true one. The principal interest of the older Upanishads consists in the absence of that systematic uniformity which we find in the later systems of philosophy, and it is to be regretted that nearly all scholars who have translated portions of the Upanishads have allowed themselves to be guided by the Brahmanic commentators. The commentators wrote all, more or less, under the influence of philosophical systems, and thought themselves justified in explaining the Upanishads in such a manner that they should agree, even in the most minute points, with the Sutras of the philosophical schools. But the authors of the Upanishads were poets rather than philosophers. Truth itself assumed, in their eyes, an aspect varying ac- UPANISHADS. 323 cording to their own feelings and misgivings. We sawthat the Bahvricha-upanishad placed Atman or the Self at the beginning of all things. The Taittiriyaupanishad1 speaks of Brahman the true, omniscient, and infinite, and derives from it the ether, the air, fire, water, earth, plants, food, seed, and body.2 This, in the eyes of the later commentators, may appear substantially the same doctrine as that of the Bahvricha-upanishad. But to us it is of interest to mark the difference, and to watch the various attempts which were made to express the idea* of a creator. The Bahvrichas, by calling him Atman in the masculine, showed that they were impressed more strongly with the idea of a, personal Being; the Taittiriyas, speaking of Brahman as neuter, gave more prominence to the idea of a Power. It was an epoch in the history of the human mind when the identity of the masculine Self and the neutral Brahman was for the first time perceived, and the name of the discoverer has not been forgotten. It was Sandilya who declared that the Self within our heart is Brahman (Chhand.-up. iii. 4. 14. p. 208.), and this tenet, somewhat amplified, is quoted as "Sandilya's wisdom " by the author of the Satapatha-brahmana (x. 6. 3.). Other sages among the Chhandogas3 again Bibl. Ind. vii. 56.
2 Purusha is body rather than man. Madhava says: cT^f 3 Chhand.-up. vi. 2. ; Bibl. Ind. iii. 394. ^q^ Tp)^^ Y 2 324 UPANISHADS.

speak simply of a Sat, or a Being, which desired to be many, and created the light, the light flowing into water, the water into food, and so on. The Atharvanikas speak of the Creator as Akshara, and it must remain doubtful whether they connected with this word the idea of the Indestructible or of Element.1 The term used by the Vajasaneyins is Avydkrita, or the Undeveloped. Every one of these terms had originally a meaning of its own, and though in later times they may all be used synonymously, they ought to be kept distinct when we are tracing the history of the human mind. Some of the ancient sages, after having arrived at the idea of Avyakrita, Undeveloped, went even beyond, and instead of the Sat or to oj/, they postulated an Asat, to pr) ov, as the beginning of all things. Thus we read in the Chhandogya-upanishad 2 : " And some say, in the beginning there was Asat (not being), alone, without a second; and from this Asat might the Sat be born."

But in spite of the great variety of philosophical thought on this and similar subjects that was to be found in the Upanishads, the want of new Upanishads was felt by the sects which sprang up in every part of fT^S^rrn fT%5T XN?T *T1? fit T^rT^fw <T^"- 1 See Gold stacker's Dictionary, s. v. Madhava says : ^C^Tf 2 Chhand.-up. vi. 1. ?T^f ^l3K<i\^*TO ^mff^m^Tf^rfN WITUfT: ^^TT^rTI UPANISHADS. 325 India.1 The old Upanishads, however, were not rejected, and to the present day the ten which are chiefly studied in Bengal are the Brihadaranyaka, the Aitareya, Chhandogya, Taittiriya, Isa, Kena, Katha, Prasna, Mundaka and Mandukya-upanishads. Every one of these has been published, and we possess an excellent edition both of text and commentary by Dr. Roer in the volumes of the Bibliotheca Indica. The whole number of Upanishads, however, known to be or to have been in existence, is much larger. It was commonly stated at 62 2, but it has lately been brought as high as 108 3, and even higher. Some of the titles given in various lists belong most likely to smaller portions of certain Upanishads, and these extracts, adopted by some sect or other, were afterwards quoted as independent treatises.4 Many are of very modern origin, and have no right to be mentioned in connection with Vedic literature. In order, however, to have this whole mass of literature together, every work that claims the title of Upanishad on any ground whatsoever, has been incorporated in an alphabetical list, which will be printed as an Appendix. There are several works which had to be consulted in drawing up this list. First, Anquetil Duperron's Oupnekhat5, a 1 Ward, A View of the History, Literature, and Mythology of the Hindus, ii. 61.
2 Ward, loc. cit. p. 61.
3 W. Elliot, Journal of the As. Soc. of Bengal, 1851, p. 607.

4 The Maitreyi-upanishad (29. 89.) is probably meant for the Dialogue between Yajnavalkya and Maitreyi in the Brihadaranyaka. The 6andilya-upanishad (57. 105.) seems to be a portion of the Chhandogya-upanishad.

5 Oupnekhat, id est, Secretum tegendum : opus ipsa in India rarissimum continens antiquam et arcanam, seu theologicam et philosophicam doctrinam, e quatuor sacris Indorum libris, Kak y 3 326 UPANISHADS.

work which contains the translation of fifty Upanishads from Persian into Latin. [The author of this Persian translation is supposed to be Ddrd ShaJcoh, the eldest son of Shah Jehan, and pupil of Babu Lai ; but in reality the work seems to have been performed by several Pandits, whom that enlightened prince called from Benares to Delhi, ordering them to translate some of their sacred works into Persian. Three years after the accomplishment of their work, their patron was put to death by his brother Aurungbeid, Djedjer baid, Sam baid, Athrban baid, excerptara ; ad verbum, e Persico idiomate, Samskreticis vocabulis intermixto, in Latinum conversum ; Dissertationibus et Annotationibus difficiliora explanantibus, illustratum : studio et opera Anquetil Duperron, Indicopleustse. Argentorat*, typis et impensis fratrum Levrault, vol. i. 1801 : vol. ii. 1802.

Duperron received a MS. of the Persian translation of the Upanishads from M. Gentil, the French resident at the court of Soudjaeddaulah. It was brought from Bengal to France by M. Bernier, in the year 1775. Duperron, after receiving another MS., collated the two, and translated the Persian into French (not published) and into literal Latin.

The Persian translation, of which several other MSS. exist, bears the following title in Duperron's translation: " Hanc interpretationem rwv Oupneknathai quorumvis quatuor librorum Beid, quod, designatum cum secreto magno (per secretum magnum) est, et integram cognitionem luminis luminum, hie Fakir sine tristitia (Sultan) Mohammed Dara Schakoh ipse, cum significatione recta, cum sinceritate, in tempore sex mensium, (postremo die, secundo rov Schonbeh, vigesimo,) sexto mensis rov Ramazzan, anno 1067 tov Hedjri (Christi, 1657) in urbe Delhi, in mansione nakhe noudeh, cum absolutione ad finem fecit pervenire."
zh: 梵文評注。舉例而言，如果我們取雅那卡（Janaka）的故事——他在祭祀中承諾給最有智慧的婆羅門一大筆獎賞——並將其與《百道梵書》（Satapatha-brahmana, xi. 4. 6.）中的記載與《廣林奧義書》（Brihadaranyaka）第三篇章中出現的同一主題進行比較，我們會發現在《森林書》（Aranyaka）中幾乎以與《梵書》完全相同的措辭給出了所有細節，但增添了許多補充內容，特別是關於亞吉那瓦爾基亞（Yajnavalkya）與其他婆羅門之間進行的哲學辯論，因此我們毫無疑問地必須將《森林書》視為對《梵書》的擴充和闡發。

森林書（Aranyaka）目前最主要的興趣在於其哲學。那些以奧義書（Upanishad）之名聞名的哲學篇章，幾乎是吠陀文獻中迄今仍被廣泛誦讀的唯一部分。它們包含、或據稱包含了印度各種哲學體系所依據的最高權威。不僅吠檀多（Vedanta）哲學家——其名號本身即宣稱他對吠陀的終極與目的的信仰¹——而且數論派（Sankhya）、勝論派（Vaiseshika）、正理派（Nyaya）與瑜伽派哲學家，都聲稱在奧義書中為其教說尋得某種根據，儘管其立場彼此對立。同樣的情況也適用於曾經存在且至今仍存在於印度的眾多教派。其創始人，若是他們¹「吠檀多」一詞在此尚未用其技術性意涵使用，見《泰帝利亞森林書》（Taittiriya-aranyaka）第十卷第十二節；該詩句在他處頻繁被引述。

在奧義書本身中，思想的自由與廣度確實很大，因此不難在其中為幾乎任何哲學主張的每一種色彩找到某種權威性。古老的奧義書並不聲稱給出超越「對真理的推測」，而當它們在時間流逝中被賦予啟示性質時，它們對那些自稱相信其為啟示之人容許了很大的迴旋餘地。然而，這對於印度歷史後期哲學教義的蓬勃發展仍然不夠；當古代奧義書中沒有一部能被發現以適應其目的時，新教派的創始人毫不猶豫、毫無困難地作起自己的新奧義書來。這說明了這些論著之數量龐大且不斷增長的原因。每一部新的手稿集合、每一份由本土作者給出的奧義書清單，都為先前已知的數量增添了新的文獻；而最現代的匯編似乎現在已享有與真正的古老論著相同的權威性。

最初的奧義書原本係位於《森林書》（Aranyakas）與《梵書》（Brahmanas）之中。只有一部《本集》（Sanhita）包含奧義書——即《瓦雅薩耶尼本集》（Vajasaneyi-sanhita），其中包括構成第四十卷的《伊沙奧義書》（Isa-upanishad），以及構成第三十四卷一部分的《濕婆願望》（Sivasankalpa）。然而，這非但不足以證明該奧義書的更古老年代，反而僅能用來確認整個以《瓦雅薩耶尼》（Vajasa-）之名所知的彙編集合的現代日期。
neyi-sanhita。¹ 然而，儘管真正的奧義書應當歸屬於婆羅門書（Brahmanas），尤其是那些通常稱為森林書（Aranyakas）的次要部分，但在後來的時代，奧義書獲得了更為獨立的地位，儘管它們仍聲稱特別歸屬於四部吠陀中的某一部，但這種關係變得極為鬆散與易變。
「奧義書」（Upanishad）一詞的真正語源學意義在印度已被遺忘。它通常被解釋為 rahasya 或 guhya（秘密），而且根據一種牽強的詞源學，奧義書被認為是指「透過神聖啟示消除激情或無明」。然而，該詞的原始意義必定是指坐在某人身邊以便聆聽，或為了冥想和禮拜。因此我們發現 up + sad 被用於坐下和禮拜的意涵：
梨俱吠陀第九卷第十一讚第六頌 — Namasa it lipa sidata，「以讚美之心親近他。」
《吠陀》第十卷第七十三頌第十一句。——Vayali suparna/h lipa sedur Fndram priyamedhah rishayah na/dhamanah，「具有善念的詩人懇求接近因陀羅，如同展翅飛翔的鳥類一般。」
字根 ds，與 sad（坐）具有相同的含義，若與前置詞 upa 相結合，便表達與 upa sad 相同的概念，即虔敬地靠近、崇拜之意（梨俱吠陀 x. 153. 1）。它經常被用來表示學生所佔據的位置¹ 馬希達拉主張《奧義書》的某些部分是針對佛教徒而作，這些佛教徒否認具有知覺的自我的存在，將生命稱為水泡，將知識稱為醉態。
2 Colebrooke, Essays, i. 92.

奧義書。319 當聆聽他的教師時，1 並且在如《沙達布拉赫馬納》(Sat.brāhmana) i. 3. 4. 15 等段落中清楚地表達了一種下位的地位：「tasmad uparyāsinam kshatriyam adhastad imah praja upasate」，「因此那些下面的人民（吠舍或毗舍）坐在下方，或向坐在上方的刹帝利致敬。」同一部著作中另一段落（ix. 4. 3. 3）更具決定性，其中 upanishddin 以臣民的意義被使用：「kshatraya tad visam adhastad upanishadinim karoti」，「他如此使下方的吠舍成為刹帝利的臣民。」因此幾乎毫無疑問，奧義書（Upanishad）最初的意義是**坐在教師身邊的行為**，2 恭敬地聆聽他；而它後來演變為隱含信仰，3 最終演變為真理或神聖啟示，是容易追蹤其發展步驟的。

吠陀的詩歌包含的哲學或神智學甚少，而婆羅門所謂的更高知識，不應在仙人（Rishis）的讚歌中尋求。《白雪奧義書》（Svetasvatara-upanishad）的作者說：「一個人若不知曉讚歌的那永恆之言——位於最高天界、一切提婆所融入其中的那個永恆之言——對讚歌還能做什麼呢？認識它的人，他們才是有福的。」1同樣的情感也頻繁地出現在…… --- 1 《梵經》（Pan.）iii. 4. 72評釋：*Upasito gurum bhavan*；及*upasito gurur bhavata*。
在這個意義上，奧義書（Upanishad）經常以複數形式使用，表示集會或聚會。
《詌陀奧義書》（Chhandogya-upanishad），第一卷，第一章，第九節。「人以知識、信任與信仰所行之事，方為有效。」
4 《白馬奧義書》（Svetasvatara-upanishad），羅爾編，《印度文庫》，第七卷。

但這一觀點在任何地方都不如《卡塔奧義書》（Katha-upanishad）中的一段話表達得更有力量，而那段話在許多方面都極為非凡。詩人說：「那神聖的自我（Self）不能由傳統所把握，不能由理解所把握，也不能由一切啟示所把握；惟有為他所選擇的人，才能獨自把握他；那自我選擇他的身體作為他自己的。」羅摩莫罕‧羅易（Kammohun Roy）訪問大英博物館時，發現已故的羅森博士（Dr. Rosen）正在準備《吠陀》讚歌的版本，對此感到驚訝，認為這是一項毫無用處的工作。但這位哲學家卻認為《奧義書》值得成為一個新宗教的基礎，他本人並出版了數部《奧義書》，附有注釋和譯文。他寫道：「對無形的至高存在的崇拜是由《奧義書》或《吠陀》的主要部分，以及《韋檀多》（Vedant）所專門規定的，」而如果《吠陀》的其他部分似乎與《奧義書》的純正教義相矛盾，他暗示整部著作不僅必須剝奪其權威性，而且應當被視為完全不可理解的。早期的印度人在追求真理時，發現調和最不同的、有時甚至自相矛盾的觀點並無困難；從《奧義書》中，甚至從那些真正且相對古老的《奧義書》中，可以蒐集出一組非凡的雜揉的神諭之言，這些言論都傾向於闡明哲學和宗教中最晦暗的問題——世界的創造、自我的本質——
「藉由學習一部吠陀」(ekavedasvikaranena)
3 《肯那奧義書》(Kena-upanishad)英譯本，譯者為羅摩莫罕‧羅易(Rammohun Roy)，加爾各答，1816年，第6頁。

奧義書。321 上帝、人與上帝的關係以及類似的主題。一個陳述被另一個陳述所矛盾，似乎從未被視為任何嚴肅的困難。因此我們在《光明奧義書》(Svetasvataraupanishad) 的第一節讀到：「梵是因嗎？我們生自何處？我們靠什麼而活？我們往何處去？誰的命令下我們遵循法則而行，經歷幸福與痛苦？時間是因嗎，還是自然、或法則、或機遇、或五大元素？人應該被視為萬物的源頭嗎？也不是它們的結合，因為必然存在一個獨立的自我，而即使是那個獨立的自我也對造成幸福與痛苦的東西無力。」對這類問題的回答自然是含糊而多樣的。例如，摩陀婆 (Madhava) 在其對帕剎羅 (Parasara) 的注疏中，首先引用《眾頌奧義書》(Bahvricha-upanishad)：「在開始時，這個（世界）單獨是自我，沒有其他任何東西眨眼。他思想，讓我創造諸世界，因而他創造了這些世界。」從這點可能推出絕對的自我被假設創造了一切無有。但隨即之後，摩陀婆引用了另一部奧義書，即《光明奧義書》(Svetasvatara) (IV. 10.)，其中摩耶或迷妄被稱為原理，而偉大的主宰本身則被迷妄所迷惑。這顯然是一個 322 奧義書。

對於商羯羅哲學的教義有所暗示，但馬德瓦則以不同的意義詮釋之。他主張此處所謂的大主宰（Great Lord）乃指神聖的自我，而幻妄（Delusion）僅是祂的一種力量，如同熱是火的一種力量。¹ 他並引用同一奧義書中的另一段經文（I. 3.），其中說道「具備冥想與直覺的智者看到了被祂自身特質所隱蔽的神聖自我的力量。」此同一詮釋亦被採納於吠檀多哲學的經文（Sutras）中，但這絕不意味著因此它就是真正的詮釋。較古老的奧義書的主要價值在於它缺乏那種我們在後來的哲學體系中所發現的系統化一致性，而幾乎所有翻譯過奧義書部分篇章的學者都允許自己受到婆羅門註疏者的引導，這是值得遺憾的。註疏者的著述或多或少都受到哲學體系的影響，且他們認為自己有權以如此方式詮釋奧義書，使其即便在最細微的點上也應當與各個哲學流派的經文相符。然而奧義書的作者毋寧說是詩人而非哲學家。真理本身在他們的眼中呈現出因人的感受與疑慮而變化的面貌。我們已看到《百路梨俱奧義書》（Bahvricha-upanishad）將自我（Atman）或心識置於萬物之始。《泰帝利耶奧義書》¹ 論及梵（Brahman）之為真、全知與無限，並由之衍生出乙太、空氣、火、水、土、植物、食物、種子與身體。² 在後來的註疏者眼中，這或可視為與《百路梨俱奧義書》的教義實質上相同。然而對我們而言，值得關注的是標記其中的差異，並觀察為了表達造物者觀念所做的種種嘗試。《百路梨俱》通過以陽性稱呼祂為自我，表明他們對於人格化存在的觀念印象更為深刻；《泰帝利耶》以中性而論述梵，則更加突出了力量的觀念。這是人類心智歷史上的一個時代，當陽性的自我與中性的梵之同一性首次被認知時，發現者的名字並未被遺忘。那便是三迪利亞（Sandilya），他宣言我們心中的自我即是梵（Chhand.-up. iii. 4. 14. p. 208.），而此教義略為闡發後，作為「三迪利亞的智慧」被《沙達帕達‧婆羅門書》的作者所引用（x. 6. 3.）。在《詩文奧義書派》（Chhandogas）中的其他智者們又是另一回事。³
2 普魯沙（Purusha）毋寧是身體而非人。馬德哈瓦說：*Chhand.-up. vi. 2.*；*Bibl. Ind. iii. 394.* 2 第324頁 奧義書。

簡單地說，有一種薩特（Sat）或存在者，它渴望成為多數，因而創造了光；光流入水，水流入食物，如此等等。雅闥婆派（Atharvanikas）將造物主稱為阿剎剌（Akshara），但這個詞是與「不可摧毀者」的觀念相連，還是與「元素」的觀念相連，仍然值得存疑。¹ 瓦賈桑耶派（Vajasaneyins）所用的術語是阿維亞克利塔（Avyakrita），意為未顯現者。這些術語中的每一個在最初都有其獨特的含義，雖然在後來的時代它們可能都被同義地使用，但當我們追溯人類心靈的歷史時，應該將它們區分開來。某些古代智者在抵達未顯現者（Avyakrita）的觀念之後，甚至更進一步，不再將薩特或存在者（to oj/）作為萬物之始，反而設定了阿薩特（Asat），非存在者（to pr) ov），作為萬物的開端。因此我們在《讚德歌奧義書》（Chhandogya-upanishad）² 中讀到：「有些人說，最初只有阿薩特（非存在），獨一無二；而薩特（存在）可能從這個阿薩特而生。」

但儘管奧義書中存在著關於此類主題的各式各樣的哲學思想，然而在印度各地興起的教派仍然感受到需要新的奧義書。舊的奧義書並未被棄絕，直至今日，孟加拉地區主要研究的十部奧義書是《大森林奧義書》（Brihadaranyaka）、《艾達雷亞奧義書》（Aitareya）、《歌者奧義書》（Chhandogya）、《泰帝利亞奧義書》（Taittiriya）、《伊沙奧義書》（Isa）、《肯那奧義書》（Kena）、《卡塔奧義書》（Katha）、《普拉什那奧義書》（Prasna）、《蒙達卡奧義書》（Mundaka）以及《曼都基亞奧義書》（Mandukya-upanishads）。這些奧義書每一部都已出版，我們擁有羅爾博士（Dr. Roer）在《印度古籍叢刊》（Bibliotheca Indica）卷冊中所編之文本與注疏的精良版本。然而，已知存在或曾經存在過的奧義書總數要龐大得多。通常說法是六十二部，但最近已提高到一百零八部，甚至更多。在各種列表中給出的某些名目很可能屬於某些奧義書的較小段落，這些摘錄被某個教派或另一教派所採用，隨後便被引用為獨立的論著。許多奧義書來自極為近代，根本無權與吠陀文獻並提。然而，為了將這整個文獻堆積編纂在一起，凡是以任何理由聲稱具有奧義書名號的著作，均已納入一份按字母順序排列的清單，該清單將作為附錄印行。在編製這份清單的過程中，須以查閱數部著作。首先是安克蒂爾‧杜佩隆（Anquetil Duperron）的《烏普尼克哈特》（Oupnekhat），
2 Ward，同上，第61頁。
3 W. Elliot, 《孟加拉亞洲學會期刊》(Journal of the As. Soc. of Bengal), 1851年, 第607頁。
4 《麥特雷耶奧義書》（Maitreyi-upanishad, 29. 89.）很可能是指《白馬奧義書》（Brihadaranyaka）中雅吉尼瓦爾基亞與麥特雷耶的對話。《桑地爾亞奧義書》（Sandilya-upanishad, 57. 105.）似乎是《詌德歌奧義書》（Chhandogya-upanishad）的一部分。
《奧義書秘義》(Oupnekhat，即應隱瞞之秘密)：此為印度極其罕見之著作，蒐集自印度四部聖典，包含古代秘義即神學與哲學之教說。

該著作包含五十部奧義書從波斯語譯成拉丁文的譯本。[這部波斯語譯本的作者據說是達拉‧希基（Ddrd ShaJcoh），沙賈汗的長子，巴布‧萊（Babu Lai）的學生；但實際上這項工作似乎是由幾位班智達（Pandit）完成的，那位開明的王子從貝拿勒斯召喚他們到德里，命令他們將一些聖典譯成波斯語。在他們完成工作三年後，他們的贊助人被其兄奧朗則布（Aurung-beid）所殺］ 傑德傑爾‧拜德（Djedjer baid）、三‧拜德（Sam baid）、阿特班‧拜德（Athrban baid）、摘錄；逐字地，從波斯慣用語言，摻雜梵文詞彙，轉譯成拉丁文；用論文與註釋闡明較為困難之處，加以闡述：由印度遊客（Indicopleustse）安克蒂爾‧杜佩隆（Anquetil Duperron）研究與編著。阿根廷*，萊夫羅特兄弟活字版與經費，卷一 1801 年；卷二 1802 年。
杜佩隆從法國駐蘇賈丁德里亞宮廷的代表紳德先生處獲得了《奧義書》波斯文譯本的手稿。這份手稿由貝爾尼埃先生於1775年從孟加拉帶到法國。杜佩隆在獲得另一份手稿後，對兩份文獻進行了校勘，並將波斯文譯成了法文（未出版）和逐字拉丁文譯本。
波斯文譯本現存數份手稿，杜佩隆的譯文中標題如下：「此乃《吠陀》四部經典中任何一部之《奧義書》的譯注，該經典以大祕密（藉由大祕密）標記，並包含光中之光的完全認識；此禁慾者（蘇丹）穆罕默德‧達拉‧沙科赫以正確的解釋、真摯的態度，於六個月期間（最後一日，沙翁貝月第二周第二十日），赫吉萊曆一〇六七年（基督曆一六五七年）拉瑪贊月第六日，在德里城那赫‧努德赫宅邸中完成此譯，並為其劃上圓滿的句點。」
